intro:
Seed (Hebrew zera'; Greek sperma) carries two intertwined senses in Scripture: the literal grain that is buried, dies, and rises into fruitful life, and the figurative "seed" meaning offspring or descendants. From these two senses the Bible builds one of its deepest patterns — life out of death, and a promised line that runs to Christ.

The image is planted in creation, where God makes plants "bearing seed according to their kinds" (Genesis 1:11–12). It becomes a thread of promise immediately after the fall: God tells the serpent that the woman's seed will crush his head while he strikes its heel (Genesis 3:15) — the first announcement of a coming deliverer. The promise narrows through the patriarchs: to Abraham God swears, "in your seed shall all the nations of the earth be blessed" (Genesis 22:18; cf. Genesis 12:7), and to David he pledges an everlasting throne for his seed (2 Samuel 7:12). Isaiah foresees that the suffering Servant, after pouring out his soul in death, "shall see his offspring" and prolong his days (Isaiah 53:10).

This is a true type, and its fulfillment is Christ. Paul reads the promises with care: the word "seed" is singular, and "it does not say, 'And to seeds,' referring to many, but… 'And to your offspring,' who is Christ" (Galatians 3:16). Jesus is the promised seed of the woman, of Abraham, of David — the one in whom the blessing reaches every nation. He also fulfills the agricultural sense of the image, applying it to his own death: "unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, it remains alone, but if it dies, it bears much fruit" (John 12:24). Buried in the ground and raised, the single grain becomes a great harvest.

From the risen Seed flows a multiplied offspring. All who belong to him become Abraham's seed and heirs of the promise (Galatians 3:29), born again "not of perishable seed but of imperishable, through the living and abiding word of God" (1 Peter 1:23). In his parables the seed sown is that very word, falling into hearts to yield a hundredfold (Mark 4:14; Luke 8:11). And the pattern reaches the believer's own future: the body is "sown perishable" and "raised imperishable," as a bare kernel is raised into glory (1 Corinthians 15:42–44). The seed motif thus gathers the whole gospel: the promised offspring who, by dying and rising, crushes the serpent, blesses the nations, and brings a great harvest of resurrection life.
